data.title: AAR Corp.: Strong Buy On Aerospace Aftermarket And MRO Growth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
AAR Corporation (NYSE:AIR) delivered a strong Q1 2026 earnings beat, demonstrating the company’s momentum in the Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul, or MRO, market. Since my last investor report, the stock has surged 27.6%, significantly outperforming the S&P 500’s 12.6% gain. In this article, I review the latest results, assess key risks and opportunities, and update my price target to support my continued buy rating on AAR Corporation.

AAR Sales Double-Digit Growth Is Not Enough For Wall Street

First quarter sales rose from $652.2 million to $739.6 million, beating analyst estimates by $50.2 million. Total sales were up 13% and 17% organically. The difference between absolute growth and organic growth is driven by the divestiture of the landing gear overhaul business in April 2025. Adjusted EBITDA increased 18% to $86.7 million, indicating a 40-basis point expansion in margins to 11.7%. Operating income grew 21% to $71.6 million, indicating margins of 9.7%, up from 9.1% a year earlier. Adjusted EPS grew 27% to $1.08 and beat analysts's expectations by $0.10. So, the company delivered a strong beat on the top and bottom lines.

The company reports its earnings through three segments:

  1. Parts Supply, which includes new parts and used serviceable materials.
  2. Repair & Engineering, which includes the company’s MRO activities.
  3. Integrated Solutions, which provides management and logistics support.

Part Supply sales grew 27% to $318 million, while adjusted EBITDA grew 34% to $43.8 million, indicating margin expansion from 13.1% to 13.8%. New parts distribution grew 20% across the commercial and government markets, while USM, or used serviceable material sales, ticked up. Repair & Engineering sales were down 1% to $214.6 million, driven by the divestiture of the Landing Gear business. Excluding this, the growth would have been 8%. Adjusted EBITDA grew 1% to $28.1 million, with margins expanding from 12.8% to 13.1%. Integrated solutions sales increased 10% to $185 million, driven by government and defense sales increases. Adjusted EBITDA grew 5% to $14.2 million, with margins sliding 30 basis points to 7.7%.

I previously noted that the divestiture of the landing gear business would bring the leverage down. The slide above, however, shows that sequentially this was not the case. The reason for that is that the company has invested in feedstock to fuel its USM business ahead. We do note that generally the net leverage has been improving.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For AAR?

The main opportunities for AAR are continued demand for MRO and parts, both new and used. Currently, there still is a shortage of airplanes that requires airlines to keep existing airplanes in the fleet for a longer time, and those airplanes require maintenance. That fuels demand for parts and services. The drawback is that to get used serviceable materials, AAR has to buy and sell airplanes. Since the airplanes are now staying longer in service, the supply is somewhat limited, and that drives up costs. Simply speaking, it means the feedstock costs are higher, and AAR has to compete with other parties for those sales. So, the company has to assess case by case whether buying a certain airplane or engine makes sense to fuel their USM business.

Another opportunity is the increase of capacity. The company is working towards adding 15% capacity in 2026, which should drive upside for the business. A possible pressure could eventually become that as airplane supply from the original equipment manufacturers improves, the highly profitable aftermarket sales growth may taper. That could provide a short-term hiccup in growth, but for the long term, a growing global fleet will drive long-term growth in sales and earnings.

AIR Stock Upgraded To Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I am upgrading AAR Corporation stock to a strong buy with a $102.04 price target, indicating 30% upside. This is driven by upward revisions of a couple of percent in the coming years for EBITDA and CAGR of around 9%. Free cash flow expectations have been revised downward, and as a result, net debt to EBITDA is expected to be 2x instead of 1.9x previously. However, we also see a credible path for the company to return to service its debt maturing in 2028, as free cash flow is still expected to grow substantially from prior levels. The 2029 (or 2028) maturities likely still need to be refinanced in part, but we see a path for the company to achieve its 1.4x EV/EBITDA as we also saw in FY23. Combining that with the upside and discount to the peer group, I believe that an upgrade to strong buy is justified.

Conclusion: AAR Corporation Stock Has Significant Upside

While free cash flow generation has been revised downward, I do believe that the company has a credible path towards reducing its leverage while the company should be able to grow its free cash flow substantially. So, I believe that the company is well positioned to service market demand and return value for shareholders, all while being significantly undervalued compared to aerospace and defense peers.
